nativeRange.habitat: Mixed coral and rubble areas of both lagoon and seaward reefs at depths of roughly 4–55 m, often near caves and overhangs.

diet.notes: Primarily feeds on benthic algae in the wild but is also documented taking microcrustaceans and detritus. In captivity, has been reported to accept Spirulina, marine algae sheets, high-quality angelfish preparations, mysis and frozen shrimp, and other meaty fare. Continuous browsing on live rock is generally considered beneficial, so multiple small feedings per day tend to be recommended over a single large meal.

avoidWith: other dwarf angelfish in small tanks
conspecifics outside of an established harem
very timid or slow feeders that may be outcompeted

summary: The Tibicen Angelfish (Centropyge tibicen) is the largest member of the Centropyge genus, reaching up to 19 cm as an adult. Native to Western Pacific reefs, it is generally considered an intermediate-level fish best kept in a spacious, well-established aquarium with plenty of live rock to graze on.
careNotes:
The Tibicen Angelfish, also sold under the names Keyhole Angelfish and Melas Angelfish, is the largest member of the Centropyge genus. Adults commonly reach 14–19 cm — substantially larger than most dwarf angelfish — and a tank of at least 400 L is generally considered appropriate for long-term care of an adult specimen. Aquascaping with ample live rock for grazing alongside open swimming space is broadly recommended.

This species is commonly considered reef safe with caution. Individual fish have been reported to nip at large-polyp stony corals, soft coral polyps, zoanthids, and clam mantles, and the risk in a reef dominated by stony corals is generally considered non-trivial. Behavior tends to be individual-specific.

In the wild, Centropyge tibicen forms harems of 3–7 individuals with one dominant male. Like other members of the genus, it is a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite — meaning individuals are born female and the dominant fish transitions to male over time, a process reported to take roughly 29–35 days. No reliable external method of distinguishing males from females has been documented in the aquarium trade.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of the Tibicen Angelfish is rarely achieved and generally considered very difficult. The species is a protogynous hermaphrodite, meaning females can transition to male under the right social conditions, with sex reversal reported to take roughly 29–35 days. No reliable external characteristics distinguish males from females. The harem structure the species forms in the wild is not readily replicated in home aquariums.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Pomacanthidae, order Acanthuriformes), max size (19.0 cm TL), temperature range (24.5–29°C), distribution (Christmas Island east to Fiji, southern Japan south to Lord Howe Island), depth range (4–55 m), and harem/protogynous biology. Temperature differs from LiveAquaria; averaged for the recorded 23–27°C range.

additional.name: ReefApp
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size 19 cm. Reports minimum tank volume 400 L vs LiveAquaria's 70 gal (265 L); the larger figure was selected for the recorded minimum given the species' adult size and activity. Considers the species not reliably reef-safe, in tension with LiveAquaria's 'with caution'.

additional.name: The Aquarium Wiki (via web search excerpt)
additional.notes: Direct fetch was blocked by access-denied page (Anubis); only used the search-result excerpt, which gave 75 gal (~284 L) minimum, 22.2–25.6°C, pH 8.1–8.4, salinity 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12. Cross-checked water parameters.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding is rarely achieved and generally considered very difficult. The species is protogynous — individuals are born female, and the dominant fish in a group transitions to male over time — with sex reversal reported to take roughly 29–35 days. No external characteristics reliably distinguish males from females.
